stories_covered: Brex acquired by Capital One for $5.15B (50% cash, 50% stock)
Anthropic's inference costs 23% higher than expected despite economies of scale
TikTok deal finalized: US investors get 80%, algorithm under Chinese control
Navan public market trading as comp to Brex acquisition
Ramp continuing to compete with Brex in business payments
Open Evidence's $12B valuation raise in 2021 context
Salesforce awarded $5.6B US Army contract over 10 years
SaaS headwinds: seat contractions, pricing pressure, AI budget cannibalization
harry_takes: A $5B exit is 'forever' money; the bad feelings about the $12B valuation drop only last a day
Brex had to raise at market price in 2021; the subsequent down-exit is the tax you pay for competitive parity back then
Capital One overpaid on paper but is strategically shrewd because they own Discover (closed-network interchange capture)
Brex founders comparing themselves to Navan comps should realize public markets won't deliver unicorn multiples; staying private longer may be wiser
Anthropic's inflation in inference costs proves the productivity-paradox: as AI gets better, end-users burn infinite tokens, costs always rise
SaaS is not dead; a $5.6B Army contract to Salesforce proves enterprise software retains defensibility
harry_boldest_quote: The bad feelings last for a day. The five billion lasts forever.
predictions: Jason Lemkin: Non-AI-attached SaaS companies must accept 30-40% slower growth and grind toward profitability; no venture capital will fund them
Rory O'Driscoll: TikTok at 1X revenue ($15-16B revenue, acquired at ~$15B) will look wildly cheap if a strategic buyer emerges at 3-5X within 3-5 years
Consensus: 2026-2027 will see a flood of 2021 unicorns IPO or M&A down-rounds as valuations clear the backlog; one per business day is realistic pace
Jason Lemkin: Inference cost optimization is a losing game; competitors will match feature-for-feature by burning more tokens if needed
disagreements: Lemkin vs O'Driscoll on Navan's IPO prospects: Lemkin suggests down-IPO signals venture capitulation and questions whether Navan will ever reach high multiples. O'Driscoll says price clears markets and believes Navan can still compound at 20-30% and trade up over 5-10 years.
harry_role: Harry drives the thesis that Brex is a clean exit despite investor disappointment, challenges the venture-wide belief that SaaS is moribund, and highlights the structural advantage of closed-network payment processors (Capital One + Discover). He moderates the late-stage pricing debate and grounds it in rational economics.
notable: Brex's sale crystallizes 'hubristic financing': you raise at peak valuation because you need capital and competitors are doing it, then accept a lower exit later—the bad feelings are rational but temporary, while the $5B outcome is permanent and generational wealth for founders.
